does: This paper investigates prompt injection attacks against LLM-based résumé screening systems through controlled experiments. The authors define prompt injection as subtle self-promotional language that doesn't introduce fake qualifications but aims to manipulate LLM rankings. They systematically evaluate injection effectiveness across two key dimensions: candidate quality homogeneity and injection prevalence. The main findings show that prompt injection significantly improves applicant rankings when few candidates inject and quality differences are minimal, but its advantage rapidly erodes as injection becomes widespread. In heterogeneous quality settings, injection occasionally allows weaker candidates to outrank stronger ones, creating fairness problems. The research identifies LLM screening's vulnerability window as occurring when manipulation is rare and candidate quality is similar.
connects: This work builds on emerging research examining adversarial attacks and prompt manipulation in LLM applications, extending prior studies of prompt injection from general chatbot contexts to the specific hiring domain. It relates to broader literature on algorithmic bias and fairness in automated decision-making systems, which has shown that AI systems can produce unfair outcomes even without intentional manipulation. The paper also connects to research on the robustness of LLMs against adversarial inputs and strategic user behavior. Additionally, it engages with computational hiring systems literature, which has documented how algorithmic screening can perpetuate discrimination. The work bridges concerns about both technical vulnerabilities in LLMs and the sociological reality of strategic behavior in high-stakes employment contexts.
enables: This research enables development of defense mechanisms for LLM-based hiring systems, such as prompt injection detection methods or robust evaluation frameworks that maintain ranking consistency despite manipulation attempts. It motivates investigation into how to design fair hiring systems that resist both accidental vulnerabilities and deliberate gaming. The findings could inform regulatory approaches to algorithmic hiring, suggesting requirements for transparency and manipulation resistance. Future work might explore detection of prompt injection in real résumés, develop better screening architectures using ensemble methods or adversarial training, or study how to preserve meritocratic ranking despite widespread manipulation. The work also raises questions about using LLMs for high-stakes decisions generally, pointing toward research on more robust and auditable alternatives for consequential human evaluation tasks.
